| Garam Masala | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Category | Herbs and spices |

Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients  | Spices and herbs

**Garam masala**, literally "hot spice" in Hindi,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) is a term for a mixture of spices used in South Asian cooking.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

Despite the modern understanding of "hot", garam masala does not typically contain chiles.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) Instead, it contains a blend of warming spices—commonly cinnamon, cumin, cardamom, coriander, clove, and peppercorn form the base.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) Nutmeg, tej pat, fennel, star anise, mace, ginger, and mustard seed may also be added according to the cook's preference. Note that regional variations exist, and even individual families may have their own blends.

Typically, the spices are toasted whole in order to develop a more complex flavor before combining and grinding to a powder.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4) A "wet" garam masala refers to these spices when combined with liquid, such as water or coconut milk.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Garam masala blends may be available at your local grocery store or market.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) However, you can also easily prepare your own blend and store at home.[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) This ensures freshness and means you have control over the quality and quantities of the individual spices.

Like all ground spices, garam masala will lose its aroma and flavor over time. However, it is shelf-stable at room temperature—store it in an airtight container away from light, heat, and moisture.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

This blend is often used to flavor meat dishes and some rice dishes.[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)
